items.title: L’armée malienne affirme avoir repris l’initiative dans les zones de combat avec le JNIM et le FLA
items.description: L'armée malienne vient d'annoncer avoir repris l'initiative et le contrôle de la situation dans toutes les zones visées par les attaques menées par le Front de libération de l'Azawad dans le nord et par le groupe « Soutien de l'islam et des musulmans », lié à Al-Qaïda, dans le centre et le sud du pays. Dans un communiqué officiel, les FAMA ont indiqué que les affrontements s’étaient poursuivis dimanche à Anifif, dans le nord-est du Mali, à environ 110 kilomètres au sud de la ville de Kidal, après l’arrivée de renforts humains et matériels destinés au Front de libération de l’Azawad. Le communiqué de l’armée malienne a ajouté : « face à cette tentative de pression, les unités des Forces armées maliennes ont poursuivi le combat avec détermination et ténacité, et ont mené les combats tout au long de la journée avec courage et discipline. » Il a réaffirmé sa « pleine confiance dans la capacité des Forces armées maliennes à défendre l’unité et l’intégrité du territoire national, à protéger la population et à vaincre les forces ennemies. » Le porte-parole du Front de libération de l’Azawad, Mohamed Mouloud Ramadan, avait annoncé hier à Sahara 24 qu’ils contrôlaient la situation à Anfif après la reprise des affrontements. Il a également démenti la mort du dirigeant du Front, Mbarek Ag Akli, et a affirmé que celui-ci continuait de diriger les opérations militaires sur le terrain dans le nord du pays.

items.title: A timeline of when politics intruded on the FIFA World Cup pitch
items.description: US President Donald Trump’s phone call to FIFA chief Gianni Infantino to have the red card for the co-host’s star striker Folarin Balogun reviewed is the latest instance of political interference in the World Cup. Balogun is free to play later on Monday in the last-16 match with Belgium after the governing body said the ban will be suspended for a year. AFP Sport highlights other examples at previous World Cups: 1934 — Mussolini’s Cup Italy’s fascist dictator Benito Mussolini made the most of the country hosting the finals. Mussolini saw the possibility of not only providing the Italian people with ‘bread and circuses’ as Roman emperors had done with gladiatorial combats in the Colosseum but also to both promote his regime and football as epitomising ‘the new Italian’. There was not much sporting about his behaviour during the tournament, attending every match and paying unannounced visits to the referees’ changing rooms. He obtained what he wanted, an Italy win, though a cloud hung over it owing to suspicions of the officiating in their matches. “This tournament was organised by Mussolini, not by FIFA,” Jules Rimet, the then-FIFA president, observed dryly. 1938 — Dictator double whammy This time round Mussolini had competition from Nazi dictator Adolf Hitler for the finals hosted in France. The Germans had annexed Austria in the Anschluss in March that year and into their hands fell a set of talented footballers who made up the national side and were known as the ‘Wunderteam’. The majority of the Austria side were far from enthusiastic to play for their new masters — a ‘friendly’ in April between the two ended with the Austrians beating Germany 2-0, in front of Hitler. There was to be no fairytale ending as far as Hitler’s regime was concerned in France — Germany exited in the first round losing 4-2 to Switzerland. Instead it was Italy who were to prevail again — the team wearing black shirts akin to those worn by Mussolini’s militia. The Italian leader was not big on subtlety, delivering a chilling message to the Azzurri on the eve of the final against Hungary: “Win or die”. The players stormed to a 4-2 win over the Hungarians. “I may have conceded four goals, but I saved their lives,” remarked Hungarian goalkeeper Antal Szabo. 1978 — Junta agreement? The tournament took place in Argentina that was in the midst of ‘The Dirty War’ as the military junta led by Jorge Rafael Videla dealt summarily with opponents — throwing some of them into the sea from helicopters and torturing others, often resulting in their death. The Higher School of Mechanics of the Navy (ESMA) in Buenos Aires — the torture centre — was close to the Monumental Stadium where the final was held. FIFA, then run by Brazilian Joao Havelange, turned a deaf ear to objections that such a regime should host football’s showpiece event. On the pitch, the hosts were struggling to deliver the home victory that Videla and his fellow generals so desperately wanted. In order to leapfrog Brazil in the table and advance to the final, they needed to beat a talented Peru team by at least four goals. The Peruvians, though, had run out of steam after topping their first round group which included eventual finalists, the Netherlands, and were out of contention to make the final. Nevertheless, eyebrows were raised as Argentina eased to a 6-0 whipping of Peru. There were whispers of a deal between the respective juntas in Argentina and Peru. Such suspicions about Argentine chicanery were not just confined to that match. “Everything, even the air, is in favour of Argentina,” remarked Hungary coach Lajos Baroti. Argentina were to go on and beat the Dutch after extra time in the final. 1982 — The whistleblower Trailing 3-1 to a flair-filled France side in their second group match, Kuwait appeared to have conceded a fourth when Alain Giresse netted for the French. However, pandemonium broke out as the Kuwaiti players claimed they thought they had heard the whistle and stopped playing. They demanded the goal be chalked off. Adding unexpected weight to their cause, the Kuwaiti Olympic Committee president Sheikh Fahad Al-Ahmed Al-Jaber Al-Sabah came on to the pitch and told the Soviet referee Myroslav Stupar to cancel the goal. Stupar acceded to his demand — it mattered little as the French were to add a fourth a minute from time. The Sheikh met a tragic end eight years later, shot dead by Iraqi troops when they invaded Kuwait.

items.title: Tchad : des missions d’audit de contrôle et de vérification à l’ONAMA et à la CNAAR
items.description: © (DR) Dans le cadre de ses missions de contrôle des institutions publiques, l’Autorité Indépendante de Lutte contre la Corruption (AILC) poursuit ses actions visant à renforcer la transparence et la bonne gouvernance. Ce 06 juillet 2026, la contrôleur générale Adjointe, Fatimé Abdelkérim Soumaïla, a d’abord procédé à la présentation de la mission à l’Office National des Médias Audiovisuels (ONAMA), relevant du ministère de la Communication. La délégation a été reçue par le ministre de la Communication, Gassim Chérif. Par la suite, la délégation s’est rendue au ministère de l’Action sociale, de la Solidarité et des Affaires humanitaires, afin d’installer la mission chargée d’examiner la gestion de la Commission Nationale d’Accueil et de Réinsertion des Réfugiés (CNAAR). La séance s’est tenue en présence de Adoum Attor Annour, Secrétaire général dudit ministère. Ces missions ont pour objectif de vérifier la conformité des procédures de gestion de l’ONAMA et de la CNAAR aux règles en vigueur, à travers un contrôle rigoureux et la vérification de la régularité des pratiques. L’article Tchad : des missions d’audit de contrôle et de vérification à l’ONAMA et à la CNAAR est apparu en premier sur Journal du Tchad .

items.title: The Military Pete Hegseth Wants
items.description: Learn more about your ad choices. Visit megaphone.fm/adchoices Subscribe here: Apple Podcasts | Spotify | YouTube | Overcast | Pocket Casts One month after his return to office, President Trump purged the senior ranks of the U.S. military. He started at the very top. Charles “C. Q.” Brown Jr., a four-star general and fighter pilot who became the second Black person confirmed as the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff in 2023, was no longer a good fit for the job. Nor was Admiral Lisa Franchetti, the first woman to lead the Navy. Several top lawyers across the military were pushed out too. “This is a reflection of the president wanting the right people around him to execute the national-security approach we want to take,” Pete Hegseth, Trump’s defense secretary, said at the time. The purge quickly revealed an ideological feature of the Trump administration: Diversity is a weakness. The military has historically been a powerful force for integration in America—even as its highest ranks have been slow to reflect the racial makeup of the country. In the July issue of The Atlantic , my colleague Clint Smith wrote about what it means that the gradual, visible racial progress in the military is being degraded as Black officers are being pushed out. He talked to dozens of Black service members, who say they are questioning their sacrifices. On Radio Atlantic , Clint joins me to discuss the legacy of Black military service and what the Trump administration’s changes could mean for the military—and America’s—future. The following is a transcript of the episode: Clint Smith: One of the impacts of Black people leaving the military at the rate that they seem to be leaving, or not even entering the military necessarily at the same rates, is that you are stripping from Black Americans one of the most effective means of achieving a different level of socioeconomic status that has reverberations across society. [ Music ] Adam Harris: The military is a kind of microcosm of America, and like America, it has been defined, in some ways, by the arc of slow, visible racial progress—particularly in its top ranks. Those who have served will tell you what it takes to become a general officer: grit, discipline, sacrifice, knowledge. But when President Trump returned to office in 2025, appointing Pete Hegseth as defense secretary, the administration saw something else as a part of that equation: diversity, equity, and inclusion. Pete Hegseth: DEI is dead. We replaced it with a color-blind, gender-neutral, merit-based approach. Harris: I’m Adam Harris. This is Radio Atlantic . And this week we need to think about the story the administration is telling about the military and the nation. My colleague Clint Smith reports in the July issue of The Atlantic on The Betrayal of Black Patriots . Now, the military doesn’t just hand out birds and stars because someone is a woman or Black; they do it because the person has earned them. How is this administration’s take on DEI affecting the people who are serving in the military? And as the country moves beyond its 250th anniversary, how will that reshape the story America tells about itself? My colleague Clint joins me to discuss. Clint, welcome to the show. Clint Smith: It’s good to be here. Harris: You wrote a piece in the July issue of the magazine called “The Betrayal of Black Patriots.” And I guess, first, can you just talk a little bit about what you mean there? What has been happening to Black service members since the beginning of the Trump administration? Smith: Yeah, I think that a lot of these folks have been experiencing a sort of cognitive dissonance in the sense that on the one hand, they are being subjected to rhetoric and policy coming from the very top of this administration that suggests that they are unworthy of the positions they hold, that suggests that they are incapable of ascending to certain high-ranking offices within the military, and if they have ascended to those positions, it’s only because it suggested affirmative action, or DEI, or the remnants of a sort of quote-unquote “woke military” under Obama and Biden. And so they feel incredibly disrespected. They feel a sense of despair. They watch as their history is disregarded, as their service is belittled, and they’re pissed off. And many of them feel a desire to remove themselves from the military, to not allow themselves to be subjected to this sort of disrespect on a daily basis. But on the other hand, there is this acute sense of the history of Black service in the military. And, you know, a lot of the folks I spoke to are second, third generation in the military, and they are acutely aware of the fact that they have ancestors who fought, you know, who were freed slaves who fought for the Union, who were living in the Deep South, where people were being lynched while they were, you know, fighting for their country in World II and World War I, during the civil-rights movement, Korea, Vietnam. And so there’s always been this tension within the context of Black military service of fighting for a country that is not always necessarily fought for you or, in fact, that has fought actively against you. And so, in some sense, there’s a sense of, you know, our ancestors had it harder than this. As difficult as this moment is, as hard as it is, we are aware that this is part of what it means to be a Black member of the military, and we have a responsibility to build on the work of our ancestors and not to allow these folks who are only going to be here for a season. You know, they use all these metaphors, like: It’s a fever that’ll break one day; it’s a storm that’ll pass one day; it’s a season that will change one day. And so they’re aware of this sort of temporary nature of what they’re experiencing, even if it is incredibly difficult, and feeling as if they have a responsibility to stay, or that at least some of them have a responsibility to stay in order to preserve the work that has been done and to continue to build on it once this current administration is out. Harris: Yeah, because being Black in the military has always been a sort of—they’ve always sort of had to prove their patriotism, right? You mentioned that the administration had been sort of belittling their service. What has the administration given as reasons to explain its stance? To explain the sort of almost forced resignations, the blocked promotions? What has the administration been giving as its reasons for this current push? Smith: The ostensible reason, as promoted by Hegseth and his aides, Secretary Hegseth, is that they are moving toward a purely meritocratic system within the military, suggesting that previous iterations of the military have not been, that promotions have not been based on merit or capability, but instead have been based on tokenism, on symbolically putting people in positions that they don’t necessarily deserve, and what’s underneath that seems to be the suggestion that if a Black person is in a high-ranking position, if they are a general, if they are an admiral, if they are a lieutenant colonel, that they got there at the expense of a more deserving white man. And it’s not only Black people who are experiencing this. You know, Secretary Hegseth has a very, it seems, narrow conception of what a “warrior ethos” looks like. And it seems to be the case that Black people and women, LGBTQ individuals, people from a range of different historically marginalized and oppressed backgrounds, do not necessarily fit into that. And what’s interesting, and I think what people don’t fully understand is that, like, these folks who Secretary Hegseth has struck from the promotion list, as he’s done over and over again at this point, in a way that’s pretty unprecedented in sort of modern administrations—they go through a really robust military board that is essentially going over their career, going over their service, going over their deployments, their combat; they’re getting character references. And so for someone to go through this process and to be recommended for promotion to a one-star general, to an admiral, whatever the case may be, these aren’t decisions that are made on a whim. And these are not decisions that are made singularly based on whether someone is going to check a box or fit into a sort of identity marker that the military service needs. They are people who are being rewarded and promoted in a way that is commensurate with the service that they have given to this country. And yet, Secretary Hegseth, you know, and there’s this question of, like, I can’t really get into Secretary Hegseth’s head, but in some ways I don’t need to, because he’s written so much of it down, and he’s said so much of this in his speeches. Hegseth (in September 30, 2025, speech): “For too long we’ve promoted too many uniformed leaders for the wrong reasons. Based on their race. Based on gender quotas. Based on historic so-called firsts. This administration has done a great deal from day one to remove the social justice, politically correct, and toxic ideological garbage that had infected our department.” Hegseth (in May 23, 2026, speech): “ Our diversity is our strength : The single dumbest phrase in military history.” Smith: He thinks that if you are a woman, if you are Black, if you are someone who has promoted the importance of diversity, equity, or inclusion, then you are similarly reflecting a sort of military that he does not want to be a part of, a military that he does not want to lead. Harris: Yeah. You know, I’m a military kid. And so I have all of these memories, right, of Black generals. There weren’t that many of them; I remember two of them that I got to meet personally. So “Fig” Newton was the base commander at a base we were stationed at and then General Boddie, who I went as—literally went as—for Halloween one year. And they were both, right, sort of mentors to my dad. But what is the significance of those people who have earned those positions? What is the significance of that diversity in the upper ranks of the military? Smith: Well, this is the thing. I mean, one: Purely on an—obviously—on a moral and ethical level, it is wrong to prevent someone from being promoted to a certain position because they have promoted themselves the importance of inclusion, empathy, generosity, and a thorough examination of history and how history shapes both the military and the sort of larger country that you are tasked with defending. But it’s also the fact that now the military is in fact a less robust, less healthy, less effective institution to do the work it is tasked with doing, because in order to engage in the sort of myriad tasks that the military engages in across the world with a wide demographic of people, with wide groups of people from all manner of different backgrounds, even within the military, you know, to your point, the thing that came up over and over again was that a lot of these top officers, and I’m sure you and your family and your dad know this, they serve as the mentors to the younger officers. And that’s not in a sort of, like, hokey way; I mean it is literally the mentorship of the officers who have been in the service for 10, 20, 30, 40 years who help the younger officers not only navigate what it means to be in the service, what it means to—how to navigate opportunities for promotion, for education, for, you know, becoming an office. It is the thing that allows people to see that there is a space for them in the military, that there is a future for them in the military, that gives them a sense of community in an institution that can sometimes be incredibly isolating. I spoke to so many officers who talked about being the only one in the room, and over and over again they were the only one in the room. And it was the affinity groups. It was the sort of informal spaces, right? People getting together after church on a base in South Korea that allowed them to come together and, you know, share that shared culture, that shared music, that shared food. That is what sustained people’s ability to do their good work. It is what sustained morale and kept it as high as it had been. And so when you destroy affinity groups, when you destroy opportunities for people to come together and be in community and see one another and be there for one another and serve as mentors and mentees for one another, you are in fact destroying the morale of the people who are serving in your military. Harris: Yeah, so, it’s both sort of being able to actually see those people, but also, right, you’re going to the officer’s club after church with your family on the weekend to do brunch. It’s clearly a very clear memory in my mind. Smith: Yeah, and that stuff matters to people. Harris: It does. Yeah it’s ability, that ability to be in community with another person and share some of those similar stories, even down to the things that you wear around base, right? You know, thinking about the military too. The military is a microcosm of society in a lot of ways. I think about the command structure of the military a lot. I wrote this piece a couple of years ago that looked at the things that were still integrating America. And as we know, one of the biggest tells of integration is whether there is a federal force, a strong central government that is pushing on that integration, which is why the military, with its strong command structure, is one of the most diverse entities in American society, right? And so, I guess, that gets to this broader societal question about, like, if it happens to the military, what are the knock-on effects for our society? So what else can be lost alongside the military culling diversity from its ranks? Smith: This may or may not be a sort of peripheral reason or impact, rather, of what’s going on, but what is true is that the military, as you’ve alluded to, has been one of the primary mechanisms throughout American history by which Black Americans can achieve social and economic mobility. And what happened—I spoke to so many people, you know, I spoke to two dozen people for this story, and over and over again, one of the things that I heard was that those folks often became the sort of financial centerpiece of their entire family, and not just like their siblings or their children or their spouse, but entire ecosystems of their family relied on the stability that they provided. These are often folks who came from working-class backgrounds. These are often folks who were the first in their families to go to college, the first in their families to ascend to certain positions and to achieve a level of economic stability that was unseen in their family previously. And so when you create the conditions to make it so that someone has to retire early, when you create the conditions that push somebody out, you’re not only impacting that person and their immediate family, you’re actually impacting the tentacles of that decision to leave shape so many people beyond just that individual. And so one of the impacts of Black people leaving the military at the rate that they seem to be leaving, or not even entering the military necessarily at the same rates, is that you are stripping from Black Americans one of the most effective means of achieving a different level of socioeconomic status that has reverberations across society. [ Music ] Harris: When we come back: how the Trump administration is trying to rewrite the American story and what that means beyond the 250th anniversary. [ Break ] Harris: We’re thinking about anniversaries right now, right? You know, July Fourth, 250th anniversary of the signing of the Declaration of Independence. But anniversaries are really just so much about the stories that we tell ourselves. And they’re also not the end of that story, right? You celebrate the anniversary and then the next year comes. So what story, as we move into 251, right, what story is the secretary of defense, Pete Hegseth, trying to tell about America’s military and what it represents? Smith: I think the story that Secretary Hegseth is attempting to tell is one that is sort of reverting back to or hearkening back to a demographic makeup of the military that increasingly reflects the sort of country that Secretary Hegseth would like to live in, more than the country that he does live in. And I think that that is part of a larger phenomenon that is related to so much of what’s going on in this administration. Obviously, so much of the immigration policy being enacted by this administration is quite explicitly, you know, as many of our colleagues have written, attempting to make it so that America is not becoming an increasingly majority-minority nation. That is the trajectory that we were on, and that is the trajectory that Stephen Miller and many others in these positions of power would like to stop. And so you see that in our asylum policy, you see that in our policy around undocumented immigration, you see that in our policy around legal immigration. They are doing all that they can to ensure—and this is not conjecture; I mean, they’ve said this—to ensure that they maintain a level of homogeneity within America, or attempt to move back toward a level of homogeneity within America in terms of its racial makeup, in terms of its ethnic makeup, in terms of its religious makeup. And it seems to be the case that Secretary Hegseth, that his vision of the military is in line with other members of this administration’s vision of America, which is to make it increasingly white. Harris: How do you square that with a sort of broader, more inclusive story of America, right? That the power structure of America right now is trying to tell a different story than this sort of broader, more inclusive America is representing. Smith: You know, I think about these conversations that I had with these officers and these soldiers, and one of the things that kept coming up over and over again was that they said that they were fighting not necessarily for what this country is right now, but they were fighting for what this country can be. They kept invoking the Constitution. They said, I’m fighting for the Constitution. I swear an oath to the Constitution. I don’t swear an oath to Secretary Hegseth. I don’t swear an oath to President Trump. I don’t swear an oath even to, you know, my neighbor in America. What I swear an oath to is the Constitution. And what we know from the Black tradition is that our forebearers thought of the Constitution as a sort of promissory note, as an aspirational document that reflected not necessarily what America was at the time when it was signed, but reflected what America could be, that reflected what America had the potential to be. And these members of the military are keenly aware of that history and are situating themselves within that history and believe themselves to be fighting for what they want this country to be, what this country itself has suggested it aspires to be. And that they are part of an intergenerational project of Black members of the military who are trying to push this country to be the best version of itself, to be what it sought out to be in the Declaration of Independence, in the Constitution. And that is in many ways a sort of guiding light for them. Harris: Yeah. One of the refrains that I saw kind of come up again and again in the story was about America as a nation of slow but visible progress, right? The military being a microcosm of that. Will we still be able to say that the story is one of slow but visible progress? The visible racial progress being the key phrase there. Will we still be able to say that 25 years from now? Smith: You know, one guy I talked to, he talked about it as—he was like, You almost gotta think about it like your retirement portfolio in the stock market. He’s like, If you look at it every day, then you’ll go crazy, and you’ll feel like maybe we’re going backwards . Maybe you’re gonna wanna pull, you know, pull out all your money from it . He’s like, But what you do is you take the long-term view, and you don’t think about it necessarily on a daily basis or a weekly basis or even a sort of year-by-year basis. What you do is you think about it in the context of your entire tenure, in your entire life, you know, the entire life of this country. And I think there was a recognition that it may be the case that we are slipping backwards in this moment in terms of the progress that has been made in the military and that we may be slipping backwards in terms of the progress we’ve made in broader society. But there is a belief in the long view. There is a recognition again of the fact that these folks won’t be here forever and that a new administration will come in, whether Democrat or Republican, that presents an opportunity to move back toward progress in a way that this one seems not to. You know, one of the folks that I talked to in the piece, who I call Reginald, who’s an officer in the Army. I think he said, like, We built this. We, my ancestors, built this country. Like, my ancestors in the military helped build the military into what it was. And as we said at the beginning of the conversation, like, they did it when they were part of segregated units. They did it when they weren’t allowed to carry a gun, because they didn’t want Black men carrying guns. They did it when they were, you know, driving the trucks through France that brought the supplies to people on the front, even when it put them in danger from, you know, the German Air Force dropping bombs on them as they’re making their way down these French roads. And over and over again, there’s this sense of: We have done so much to defend this country. We have done so much to serve this country. My direct ancestors , they would say , their literal blood is in the soil of this country and abroad defending this country. And I’m not gonna let some people come in and push me out because they don’t like what I look like, or they don’t like what I sound like, or they think that I’m in this position simply because I’m here to fill a quota. I know the truth. We know the truth, and part of our job is to ensure that enough of us are still here once this storm has passed, as they would say , to ensure that there are people to pick up the pieces when the sky turns blue again. Harris: Yeah. Yes, it’s almost as if the ancestors did it when belief was all they had, who are we to do any different? Clint, thank you for giving us so much to think about, and thank you for joining me. Smith: Appreciate it. Always. Harris: That’s all for today’s show. This episode of Radio Atlantic was produced by Mike Pesoli and Hadley Robinson. It was engineered and has music by Rob Smierciak. Claudine Ebeid is the executive producer of Atlantic audio, and Andrea Valdez is our managing editor.

items.title: Maroc : démantèlement d’une cellule terroriste affiliée à Daech après des perquisitions effectuées dans plusieurs villes du royaume
items.description: Les autorités marocaines ont annoncé ce lundi avoir déjoué un complot terroriste qui, selon elles, en était à un stade avancé de préparation, et avoir arrêté dix personnes soupçonnées d’être liées à une cellule affiliée à l’organisation « Daech » qui opérait avec le soutien et en coordination avec la branche de l’organisation dans la région du Sahel. Le Bureau central des enquêtes judiciaires (BCEJ) a indiqué dans un communiqué que l’opération avait été menée sur la base de renseignements fournis par la Direction générale de la surveillance du territoire national (DGST) et qu’elle avait donné lieu à des perquisitions simultanées dans les villes d’Agadir, de Taroudant, de Casablanca, de El Hajeb, de Tétouan, de El Faqih Ben Saleh et de Safi. Le Bureau a ajouté que les personnes interpellées, parmi lesquelles figuraient un mineur et un individu déjà incarcéré pour des affaires liées au terrorisme, sont soupçonnées d’être impliquées dans la préparation d’attentats visant « l’ordre public et la sécurité des personnes et des biens », précisant que les enquêtes ont révélé l’existence d’une coordination logistique et d’un soutien opérationnel avec la branche de l’organisation « Daech » dans la région du Sahel. Les autorités ont indiqué que les perquisitions avaient permis de saisir des armes blanches, des tenues militaires, ainsi que des documents et des manuscrits qu’elles ont qualifiés d’extrémistes, contenant notamment des instructions pour la fabrication d’engins explosifs, en plus de supports numériques et d’enregistrements qui, selon elles, comportaient des serments d’allégeance à l’organisation « Daech » et des menaces de commettre des actes au Maroc. Les autorités ont également annoncé avoir découvert, dans un entrepôt de la ville d’Inezgane, un 4×4 dont le système d’alimentation en carburant avait été modifié. Elles ont précisé que les premières enquêtes indiquaient que ce véhicule était destiné à être utilisé dans le cadre d’une « opération terroriste » pouvant inclure un attentat-suicide ou une attaque au véhicule bélier visant des installations ou des sites sensibles. Le communiqué ajoute que les autorités ont évacué les environs de l'entrepôt par mesure de précaution, avant qu'une équipe spécialisée dans les explosifs ne procède à l'inspection du véhicule à l'aide de robots et de capteurs, en vue de le soumettre, ainsi que l'entrepôt, aux expertises techniques nécessaires. Le bureau a indiqué que la perquisition à l’intérieur de l’entrepôt avait également permis de saisir des bouteilles de gaz et des engins de pression, dont certains étaient remplis de clous ou reliés à des fils électriques, ainsi que du matériel de soudure et des produits chimiques qui, selon lui, feront l’objet d’analyses techniques afin de déterminer leur nature et leur usage. Selon le communiqué, les résultats préliminaires de l’enquête indiquent que les membres de la cellule ont prêté allégeance au chef de l’organisation « Daech » et ont reçu des instructions directes de la part de dirigeants de la branche de l’organisation dans la région du Sahel, leur enjoignant de rester au Maroc pour mener des attaques, tout en reportant leur départ vers les zones d’activité de l’organisation à l’étranger. Les autorités ont ajouté que l’enquête avait également révélé que le chef de la cellule avait réparti les rôles entre ses membres, notamment la sélection des cibles, les opérations de surveillance et de reconnaissance, ainsi que l’acquisition du matériel et de l’équipement nécessaires à la perpétration des attentats présumés. Le Bureau central des enquêtes judiciaires a indiqué que les adultes interpellés ont été placés en garde à vue, tandis que le mineur a été soumis à une mesure de surveillance, dans le cadre d’une enquête menée sous la supervision du parquet compétent en matière de terrorisme, dans le but de déterminer toutes les circonstances de l’affaire et de mettre au jour d’éventuelles ramifications de la cellule au Maroc et à l’étranger.

items.title: Médias : le journaliste Mamady Konoma Keita interdit d’exercer, KonomaTV Guinée et son site également suspendus
items.description: Le journaliste Mamady Konoma Keita est désormais interdit d’exercer le métier de journaliste sur toute l’étendue du territoire national. La décision, rendue publique par la Haute Autorité de la Communication (HAC), vise également la web TV KonomaTV Guinée ainsi que le site d’informations générales www.konomaguinee.com , qui lui sont associés. Selon la décision, Mamady Konoma Keita est accusé d’exercer illégalement la profession de journaliste. Les autorités lui reprochent également de graves manquements aux règles déontologiques ainsi que des agissements considérés comme constituant des menaces à l’ordre public. items.title: Le temps des réponses (Par Oumar Boubacar NDONGO)
items.description: Le rêve s'est arrêté brutalement. Une défaite 3-2 contre la Belgique en seizième de finale de la Coupe du monde 2026, des regrets plein la tête et un sentiment largement partagé : le Sénégal est passé à côté de sa Coupe du monde. Perdre face à une grande nation n'a rien d'infamant. En revanche, donner l'impression de ne jamais avoir pleinement préparé le rendez-vous le plus important du football mondial est une autre histoire. Et c'est précisément ce qui nourrit aujourd'hui la frustration des Sénégalais.Depuis l'élimination, un mot revient avec insistance : responsabilités. Cette campagne mondiale laisse derrière elle trop de questions sans réponses. Comment expliquer une préparation qui a semblé improvisée ? Pourquoi l'environnement de la sélection a-t-il donné l'impression d'être davantage tourné vers la célébration du sacre continental que vers la construction d'une ambition mondiale ? Pendant que d'autres affinaient leurs plans, le Sénégal semblait encore vivre sur les souvenirs de la Can. Sur le terrain, les interrogations n'ont fait que grandir. Les choix du sélectionneur Pape Thiaw ont souvent été difficiles à comprendre. Certaines compositions, certains changements et certaines orientations tactiques ont alimenté les débats bien avant l'élimination. Le doute s'est installé et n'a jamais vraiment quitté les Lions. Mais le plus inquiétant n'était peut-être pas le football. C'était l'impression que le groupe avait perdu cette force collective qui faisait sa marque de fabrique. Pendant des années, cette équipe s'est distinguée par son unité, sa solidarité et sa capacité à souffrir ensemble. Cette fois, quelque chose semblait s'être fissuré. Les déclarations de Pape Guèye, annonçant qu'il ne reviendrait pas en sélection tant que le staff ne serait pas changé, ont renforcé ce sentiment. Qu'un international en arrive publiquement à une telle position révèle un malaise qui dépasse largement le cadre d'un simple résultat sportif. Si cette fracture est réelle, elle devra être traitée rapidement. Car une sélection nationale ne peut avancer durablement dans la division. Aujourd'hui, le peuple sénégalais ne réclame pas des têtes pour le simple plaisir de désigner des coupables. Il demande des explications. Il veut comprendre ce qui n'a pas fonctionné. Où les erreurs ont-elles été commises ? Qui les assume ? Et surtout, quelles mesures seront prises pour qu'elles ne se reproduisent plus ? Le football sénégalais a suffisamment grandi pour sortir de la culture de l'autosatisfaction permanente. Les succès doivent être célébrés, mais les échecs doivent être analysés avec la même honnêteté. Une Coupe du monde ne se prépare pas quelques semaines avant le premier match. Elle se construit pendant des mois, voire des années, avec de la rigueur, de l'anticipation et une vision claire. Le temps n'est pourtant pas à la résignation. Dans un peu plus d'un an, la Can 2027 sera déjà là. Les échéances s'enchaînent vite et le luxe de perdre du temps n'existe plus. Les autorités sportives ont désormais le devoir de faire un bilan lucide, sans faux-semblants ni protection de personnes. Les responsabilités doivent être situées, les dysfonctionnements corrigés et les décisions prises dans l'intérêt exclusif du football sénégalais. Les supporters, eux, continueront d'être au rendez-vous. Ils l'ont toujours été. Dans les victoires comme dans les défaites, ils portent les Lions avec une fidélité qui ne se dément pas. Cette fidélité mérite du respect. Et le premier signe de respect consiste à dire la vérité. Le Sénégal ne grandira pas en cherchant des boucs émissaires. Il grandira en ayant le courage de regarder ses erreurs en face. Parce qu'au-delà d'une élimination, c'est la crédibilité d'un projet sportif qui est aujourd'hui en jeu. Et cette fois, le silence ne suffira pas. Qu’ils soient fédéraux, techniciens, joueurs, ou simples accompagnants de la sélection, les fautifs doivent désignés. Ce n’est pas une vindicte populaire, mais un devoir envers le peuple, pour que pareille désillusion ne se reproduise plus…

items.title: 2éme session ordinaire du Conseil des ministres de UEMOA : des décisions importantes pour la stabilité du système financier régional
items.description: Les principales conclusions de la deuxième session ordinaire du Conseil des ministres de l’année 2026 de l’Union Économique et Monétaire Ouest-Africaine (UEMOA) ont porté sur des décisions importantes pour la stabilité du système financier régional. Cette rencontre statutaire a eu lieu le vendredi 3 juillet 2026 à Ouagadougou en présence du président du Conseil des ministres, également ministres de l’Economie et des finances, Docteur Aboubacar Nacanabo. Il s’agit de: -adoption du Rapport sur la Surveillance multilatérale de juin 2026 et celle sur les orientations de politique économique des États membres pour l’année 2027 ; -adoption du Règlement établissant les procédures communautaires relatives à l’autorisation de mise sur le marché et à la surveillance des médicaments vétérinaires, ainsi que l’institution d’un Comité régional du médicament vétérinaire, constituent des avancées importantes. Derrière ces textes, il y a un enjeu très concret : protéger notre cheptel, sécuriser nos filières d’élevage, préserver la santé publique et renforcer la confiance dans notre marché régional ; -prise des décisions importantes pour le développement et la stabilité de notre système financier régional ; -L’approbation du budget 2026 de l’Autorité des Marchés Financiers de l’UMOA, ainsi que les décisions relatives au renouvellement de mandats et à la nomination de membres de son Collège ; -Les décisions relatives aux interventions de la BOAD, notamment celles concernant la Société Financière Internationale, PROPARCO et Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corporation, participent de cette même ambition. Elles permettront de renforcer les capacités de mobilisation de ressources de notre Banque commune et d’accroître ses moyens d’action. L’ambition est de faire de la BOAD un instrument toujours plus puissant au service de la transformation structurelle des économies ; -l’opérationnalisation du Fonds de Stabilité Financière de l’Union Monétaire Ouest Africaine constitue une avancée majeure. Ce Fonds renforcera notre capacité collective à anticiper les risques, à contenir les crises et à préserver la stabilité de notre espace monétaire et financier. Synthèse de RD The post 2éme session ordinaire du Conseil des ministres de UEMOA : des décisions importantes pour la stabilité du système financier régional appeared first on L'economiste du faso .

items.title: Le porte-parole de la coalition de l’opposition: dix ans de mandat présidentiel suffisent pour dresser le bilan de ce projet politique
items.description: Le porte-parole de la coalition de l'opposition démocratique, Sidi El Kory, a déclaré que les dix années de mandat du président actuel avaient suffi pour dresser le bilan de son projet politique, estimant que cette période n'avait pas permis de tirer parti de manière concrète des ressources économiques du pays. Ces déclarations ont été faites lors d’un rassemblement populaire organisé samedi par l’opposition à Nouadhibou, pour dénoncer ce qu’elle qualifie de « coût élevé de la vie ». Il a affirmé que l’opposition mauritanienne « rejette tout débat sur un troisième mandat », soulignant que le respect de la Constitution et la transmission pacifique du pouvoir « constituent la garantie fondamentale de la consolidation de la démocratie », selon ses propres termes. Ould El Kory a ajouté que la Mauritanie disposait d’importantes richesses halieutiques et minières ainsi que d’importants gisements de gaz, mais que cela « ne s’était pas traduit par un développement ni par une amélioration du niveau de vie des citoyens », selon ses propres termes. Sur le plan économique, il a indiqué que le budget général était passé d’environ 500 milliards d’ouguiyas à plus de 1 200 milliards d’ouguiyas, sans que cela ne se traduise par une amélioration des services de base ou du niveau des revenus. Il a souligné la faiblesse persistante des secteurs de la santé et de l’éducation, le recours des citoyens aux soins à l’étranger, et la faiblesse des salaires face à la hausse du coût de la vie. Il a également évoqué la hausse du chômage et l’exode des jeunes, et la flambée des prix des carburants et des denrées alimentaires, estimant que « la croissance économique n’a pas profité aux catégories les plus nécessiteuses ».

items.title: Sanguié : La montée mensuelle des couleurs marquée par la lecture du message à la Nation du Président du Faso à Réo
items.description: BURKINA-SANGUIE-REO-MONTEE-MENSUELLE-COULEURS-NATIONALES-MESSAGE-PRESIDENT Sanguié : La montée mensuelle des couleurs marquée par la lecture du message à la Nation du Président du Faso à Réo Réo, 6 juil. 2026 (AIB) – La cérémonie tournante de montée des couleurs dans la province du Sanguié s'est tenue lundi au district sanitaire de Réo, suivie de la lecture du message à la Nation du Président du Faso, par le Haut-commissaire, Talari Germaine Ouoba. La cérémonie a été présidée par le Haut-commissaire de la province, Talari Germaine Ouoba. Elle a été marquée par la lecture de l'adresse à la Nation du Président du Faso, le Capitaine Ibrahim Traoré, également Président de la Confédération des États du Sahel (AES), à l'occasion du deuxième anniversaire de la Confédération. Dans son message, le Président du Faso a salué les acquis enregistrés depuis la création de la Confédération des États du Sahel et réaffirmé la détermination du Burkina Faso, du Mali et du Niger à consolider leur union autour des idéaux de souveraineté, de sécurité, d'intégration économique et de développement au profit des populations. À la suite de cette lecture, le Médecin-chef du district sanitaire de Réo, Dr David Nikiema, a présenté son district sanitaire, qui couvre les communes de Dassa, Didyr, Godyr, Kordié et Réo, avec une population estimée à plus de 232 000 habitants. Il a exposé l'organisation de la structure, ses missions ainsi que les principales orientations qui guident ses actions en matière de santé publique. Le responsable du district sanitaire a également évoqué les difficultés auxquelles son service est confronté, notamment l'exiguïté des infrastructures administratives, le manque de magasins de stockage pour les vaccins et les médicaments, l'insuffisance de moyens roulants ainsi que les besoins en renforcement du plateau technique et des logements du personnel. En réponse, le Haut-commissaire a salué l'engagement, le professionnalisme et la résilience des agents de santé qui œuvrent quotidiennement pour la prise en charge des populations malgré les contraintes. Elle les a félicités pour les résultats obtenus et les a encouragés à poursuivre leurs efforts. La camarade Ouoba a également indiqué avoir pris bonne note des préoccupations soulevées par le district sanitaire et a rassuré que celles-ci seront examinées avec les acteurs concernés. À l'issue de la cérémonie, les autorités administratives, accompagnées des forces vives et des partenaires, ont effectué une visite des locaux du district sanitaire afin de constater les réalités évoquées par les responsables de la structure. Agence d'information du Burkina FGB/AS/ATA

items.title: Former Syrian Officials Found Guilty in Torture of Pro-Democracy Protesters
World: Attacks on Health Care Bi-Monthly News Brief, 10-23 June 2026
items.description: Countries: World, Colombia, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Ethiopia, Haiti, Lebanon, Myanmar, Nigeria, occupied Palestinian territory, Pakistan, Sudan, Syrian Arab Republic, Ukraine, Yemen Source: Insecurity Insight Please refer to the attached file. Since the adoption of United Nations Security Council Resolution 2286 in 2016 and 09 June 2026, Insecurity Insight has documented over 19,000 attacks on health care worldwide, highlighting the persistent and widespread failure to protect health care in conflict. To mark a decade since the adoption, Insecurity Insight released a landmark report analysing ten years of violence against health care. Drawing on a decade of systematically collected data, Still Under Attack documents the scale, patterns, and consequences of attacks on health care in conflict-affected settings around the world. Among the report's key findings: More than 3,860 health workers were killed and over 2,500 were arrested or detained between January 2016 and December 2025. Local health workers remain particularly vulnerable, accounting for more than 65% of documented health worker killings. The growing use of armed drones and explosive weapons is contributing to increasing damage to health facilities and to health worker deaths and injuries. Based exclusively on Insecurity Insight's data collection, the report shows how violence against health care has become increasingly normalised, with severe consequences for civilians, disease response, and the resilience of health systems. The report also provides detailed analysis of perpetrators, methods of attack, and emerging risks, including the growing role of new technologies in conflict. It concludes with recommendations for governments, international organisations, humanitarian actors, and the diplomatic community to strengthen the protection of health care and improve accountability for violations. Past editions: 27 May-09 June ; 13-26 May ; 29 April-12 May ; 15-28 April ; 01-14 April ; 18-31 March ; 04-17 March ; 18 February-03 March ; 04-17 February ; 21 January-03 February ; All SHCC Reports: 2025 ; 2024 ; 2023 ; 2022 ; 2021 ; All Help support the protection of health care by sharing this resource. Please copy and paste this link: https://bit.ly/10-23Jun2026Health SHCC Attacks on Health Care This section aligns with the definition of attacks on health care used by the Safeguarding Health in Conflict Coalition (SHCC). Africa Democratic Republic of the Congo 13 June 2026: In Watsa territory, Haut-Uele province, the head nurse at Mungbere General Referral Hospital was shot and killed by a soldier who was reportedly attempting to speak to a patient receiving treatment, and the nurse was shot while intervening to inquire about the situation and protect the patient. Source: Radio Okapi As reported on 14 June 2026: In Mongbwalu town, Djugu territory, Ituri province, health workers burying a suspected Ebola casualty were violently obstructed from accessing a coffin by residents. Police officers warned residents not to touch the coffin but were later forced to use warning shots and tear gas to allow health workers to retrieve the body. Source: Personal Communication 15 June 2026: In Wanamahika Mutiri area, Butembo city, North Kivu province, Adventist (Wanamahika) Hospital was stormed by unidentified armed men carrying bladed weapons and knives, who abducted a woman and her six-year-old daughter from the facility. The child had tested positive for Ebola. The mother and daughter were later found at an Ebola treatment centre roughly 18km from Butembo. Sources: Actualite , AOL , BBC and Reuters 16 June 2026: In Ituri province, five Ebola response workers were briefly kidnapped by armed groups. Sources: Namibia Daily News and United Nations 16 June 2026: In Kakumba village, Walungu territory, South Kivu province, two Red Cross workers wearing Red Cross emblems were killed by armed men while on a mission to supervise drinking water supply work. The armed men allegedly knew the nature of their mission in the area. Sources: ICRC and Radio Okapi Insecurity Insight’s latest social media monitoring brief analyses public narratives surrounding the 17th Ebola epidemic in the Democratic Republic of the Congo between 15 June to 22 June 2026. Key findings: Politics, not the outbreak, drove the conversation. Comment volume rose 33% on the previous period, but almost entirely on the back of a single statement by an opposition figure linking the DRC's World Cup performance to governance failures (1,160 comments, 26.5% of the total). Harmful content stayed minimal in posts and scattered in comments. A government attempt to deflect criticism onto an NGO backfired. After a health organisation warned of "dangerous gaps" in the response, the government rejected the criticism as "alarmist" and urged NGOs to show restraint. The backlash was overwhelmingly hostile to the government rather than the NGO, and dominated by the "Ebola business" corruption frame. A cluster of attacks on Ebola facilities and health workers was amplified and reframed online. The incidents are real and corroborated, but their online life was driven by a small network of accounts - and the single highest-reach post had over 400,000 views. Read the brief in English and French . Visit our website for precious briefs**.** Data from Insecurity Insight shows that since 21 May and 21 June 2026, at least 19 incidents of violence against or obstruction of health care impacting Ebola response measures were reported. Two Ebola treatment facilities were attacked and deliberately set on fire and another vandalised, damaging windows and a water supply system, compromising access to drinking water. Health workers were repeatedly assaulted, threatened and violently obstructed from performing their duties. Ethiopia 13 June 2026: In Sir Iyasus kebele, Enemay woreda, East Gojjam zone, south-western Amhara region, a health facility was looted by ENDF members during a raid in the area. Source: Amhara Association of America Nigeria 21 June 2026: In Kawel village, Mushere district, Bokkos local government area, Plateau state, a primary health centre was attacked by gunmen with guns and machetes at around 2330hrs, killing 22 people, including an unspecified number of health workers and patients. Source: Daily Trust Sudan Data from Insecurity Insight shows that since fighting started between the SAF and RSF in April 2023 and 09 June 2026 at least 761 incidents of violence against or obstruction of access to health care took place across Sudan: Over two-thirds of these 761 attacks were attributed to the RSF 211 health workers were killed, 15 kidnapped and 127 arrested Health facilities were damaged 216 times 63 attacks where armed drones affected health care 51 attacks on maternal health care services Incident briefs: 10-23 June ; 27 May-09 June ; 13-26 May ; 29 April-12 May ; 15-28 April ; 01-14 April ; 18-31 March ; 04-17 March ; 18 February-03 March ; 04-17 February ; 21 January-03 February ; 07-20 January ; All SHCC chapters (EN): 2025 ; 2024 ; 2023 ; 2022 ; 2021 . (AR): 2025 ; 2024 ; 2023 ; 2022 ; 2021 Asia Pakistan Between 05 June - 01 July 2026: In Quetta city, Balochistan province, a female doctor was attacked and severely burned with acid inside the Civil Hospital by a hospital lift operator. A medical technician was also burned when he rushed to help the doctor. The lift operator was later shot and killed by police who tracked him down at a local bus station. In response to the acid attack, doctors staged protests in Quetta. Sources: Arab News , Ians Live and The Jerusalem Post Myanmar Data collected by Insecurity Insight shows that since the military coup on 01 February 2021 and 04 June 2026 at least 1,943 incidents of violence against or obstruction of access to health care took place across Myanmar: equivalent to one attack on health care every day for more than five years 65% of these attacks were attributed to the Myanmar Armed Forces (MAF) 175 health workers were killed and 932 arrested health facilities were damaged on at least 509 occasions So far this year, at least seven health workers were killed by explosive weapons. Five health workers were killed in three incidents involving MAF airstrikes: two occurred at prisoner-of-war detention facilities under the control of opposition forces in Rakhine and Yangon, while the third involved the bombing of a hospital located in areas under the control of the PDF in Sagaing. A further two health workers were killed in two separate roadside landmine explosions reportedly attributed to opposition forces in Bago and Magway regions. Incident briefs: 10-23 June ; 27 May-09 June ; 13-26 May ; 29 April-12 May ; 15-28 April ; 01-14 April ; 18-31 March ; 04-17 March ; 18 February-03 March ; 04-17 February ; 21 January-03 February ; 07-20 January ; All SHCC chapters: Burmese: 2025 ; 2024 ; 2023 . English: 2025 ; 2024 ; 2023 : 2022 ; 2021 ; 2020 Europe Ukraine 10 June 2026: In Industrialnyi district, Kharkiv city, raion and oblast, a private dental clinic and apartments were damaged when a Russian drone fell near a residential building, sparking a fire. Source: UA Varta 11-12 June 2026: In Hlukhiv city and urban hromada, Shostka raion, Sumy oblast, a pharmacy and shops inside a shopping centre were damaged in an overnight drone attack by Russian forces. Source: Kordon Media 13 June 2026: In Bilopillia city and urban hromada, Sumy raion and oblast, a 64-year-old nurse was killed after a Russian drone hit their car around 0730hrs. Her acquaintance who was driving was also injured. The nurse worked in the interventional radiology department of the Central City Clinical Hospital of the Sumy City Council. Sources: Central City Clinical Hospital , Kordon Media , Odin Doma Sumy and The Sumy Post 15 June 2026: In Kushugum rural settlement and settlement hromada, Zaporizhzhia raion and oblast, a parked ambulance was hit by a Russian drone strike on the territory of a medical facility. A female nurse was injured and the ambulance caught fire. Sources: InformZP , MIG , Regio News , State Emergency Service of Zaporizhzhia , The Head of Zaporizkaya OVA and UA Varta 18 June 2026: In Pidserednie village, Prykolotne rural settlement, Kupiansk raion, Kharkiv oblast, an ambulance was damaged by Russian shelling. Source: UA Varta 20 June 2026: In Nikopol raion, Dnipropetrovsk oblast, a pharmacy and other civilian infrastructure were damaged in a Russian attack on the oblast with artillery, missiles and drones. Sources: 057 , Apostrophe , Censor , Dnepr Express , Dnipro Media , Dnipro News , DniproTV , DSNews , Hirchytsya , Interfax , Informator , Nikopolnews , Ukrainian Truth , Ukrinform and Vidomo 23 June 2026: In Kharkiv oblast, a medical facility, a kindergarten, residential buildings, administrative buildings, power grids and other civilian infrastructure were damaged in an attack by Russian forces. Source: UA Varta Insecurity Insight has documented over 3,000 attacks on Ukraine’s health facilities, health workers and infrastructure since the start of Russia’s full-scale invasion on 24 February 2022 to 30 April 2026, averaging nearly two attacks per day. Hospitals and clinics have been damaged or destroyed 1,632 times 462 health workers have been killed and 476 others injured. 113 attacks on services provided for women during pregnancy, childbirth and the postnatal period Explore this interactive map - a joint undertaking among Insecurity Insight, eyeWitness to Atrocities (eyeWitness), Media Initiative for Human Rights (MIHR), Physicians for Human Rights (PHR), Truth Hounds, and the Ukrainian Healthcare Center (UHC) - to see where incidents took place. Available in English and Ukrainian . Map filters include attacks on maternal and childcare services, military use of health facilities and attacks impacting energy and water supplies. SHCC Chapters: EN: 2025 ; 2024 ; 2023 ; 2022 . UK: 2025 ; 2024 ; 2023 Middle East and North Africa Lebanon 10 June 2026: In Haris municipality, Bint Jbeil district, South governorate, two Al-Risala Association paramedics were injured in an Israeli airstrike. Sources: Lebanese Ministry of Public and 26 September 10 June 2026: In Tyre city and district, South governorate, MSF temporarily suspended activities, including its mobile clinics and support to Jabal Amel Hospital, Qana Hospital and the Lebanese-Italian Hospital, following large-scale evacuation orders issued by the Israeli army covering Tyre, its camps and several surrounding areas. MSF evacuated its teams to Sidon and stated that the suspension was due to the forced displacement of residents and the deteriorating security situation. Source: Syrian Arab News Agency 11 June 2026: In Tyre district, South governorate, the vicinity of Hiram Hospital was hit by an Israeli airstrike, injuring 10 nurses and hospital staff members. Patient rooms and the emergency department were damaged, and windows were shattered in staff vehicles. Sources: Drop Site and UN News 21 June 2026: In Sidon city, Tyre district, South governorate, a pregnant nurse was killed in an Israeli airstrike. Source: Quds News Network Occupied Palestinian territory Data collected by Insecurity Insight shows that between 07 October 2023 and 09 June 2026, at least 3,327 incidents of violence against or obstruction of access to health care took place across the occupied Palestinian territory. 95% of all incidents were attributed to Israeli forces. Gaza: 2,328 attacks on health care were recorded during which health facilities were damaged 448 times, 765 health workers were killed and 398 arrested. Patient access to medicine was obstructed on 289 occasions. West Bank and East Jerusalem: 938 attacks on health care were recorded during which health facilities were damaged 30 times, 12 health workers were killed and 188 arrested. Patient access to medicine was obstructed on 579 occasions. Of the 938 attacks on health care in the West Bank, 47 were attributed to Israeli settlers, who were typically armed with guns or rocks. The proportion of such attacks more than doubled in 2025 compared to 2024, rising from around 2% to 9% of total reported attacks. Between 01 January and 09 June 2026, Israeli settler-attributed attacks increased further, accounting for 22% of all reported attacks during that period. Most Israeli settler attacks on health care targeted ambulances and first responders, including blocking their access to those needing care. On at least seven occasions, Israeli settlers attacked health care under the protection of the IDF. Incident briefs: 10-23 June ; 27 May-09 June ; 13-26 May ; 29 April-12 May ; 15-28 April ; 01-14 April ; 18-31 March ; 04-17 March ; 18 February-03 March ; 04-17 February ; 21 January-03 February ; 07-20 January ; All SHCC Factsheets (EN): 2025 ; 2024 ; 2023 ; 2022 ; 2021 . (AR): 2025 ; 2024 ; 2023 ; 2022 ; 2021 . Syrian Arab Republic As reported on 15 June 2026: In Ar Raqqa city and governorate, the vicinity of the National Hospital and Intifada School was under threat when the Interior Ministry was attacked in a suicide bombing by unidentified perpetrators, killing two employees of the Interior Ministry. One of the attackers was killed by intervening security forces, while the other was killed in the detonation. Source: Al Bilad Press Yemen 11 June 2026: In Al-Darain area, Sheikh Othman district, Aden city, two doctors who were husband and wife, both employed at Doctors' Tower Hospital, were shot and killed alongside two Yemeni soldiers, and an unspecified number of others were wounded, when a soldier guarding the home of the governor of Yemen opened fire in the morning. The soldier was later killed during an attempted arrest by security forces. Sources: Al Bilad , Al Montasaf , The Independent and Yemen Shabab The Americas Colombia 18 June 2026: In García Herreros neighbourhood, Cúcuta city, Norte de Santander department, a pharmacy was robbed and an employee threatened and hit by two men armed with pistols. Cash was stolen from the register. Source: Cucuta Online Haiti 18-19 April 2026: In Port-au-Prince city and arrondissement, MSF staff members were trapped in their homes during clashes between rival armed groups with intense gunfire. Nearly 40 people, including the MSF staff and their families, sought refuge in the MSF hospital in Cité Soleil, having no other safe option. Source: MSF 15 June 2026: In Cité Soleil, Port-au-Prince city and arrondissement, the MSF-supported Isaïe Jeanty Maternity Hospital was hit and damaged by numerous stray bullets amid crossfire between armed groups. A woman was injured in the leg by a stray bullet inside the hospital grounds. MSF suspended activities the next morning but continued to provide emergency care, patients’ stabilisation and referrals for several days. On 19 June, the organisation evacuated its staff and suspended all activities. Source: MSF Other resources Attacks on health care were relentless in 2025. Using data collected by Insecurity Insight, the Safeguarding Health in Conflict Coalition (SHCC) recorded 2,546 incidents in 33 countries in which damage to or the destruction of health care facilities was reported on at least 790 occasions, 455 health workers were killed, 218 kidnapped and 263 arrested. Key findings in 2025 Violence rose in 13 countries and recorded incidents remained far above the ten-year annual average. Continued high levels of violence by state forces alongside rising levels of violence by non-state armed actors. The expansion of remotely piloted aerial systems (drones) increasingly affected health care in conflict settings. Kidnapping of health workers increased, particularly in the Central African Republic, Colombia, the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Mali, Pakistan and Syria. The report includes detailed profiles of 21 countries and territories where many acts of violence against health care by state parties and non-state armed groups, including organized criminal groups, took place. These include: Africa: Burkina Faso , Cameroon , the CAR , the DRC , Ethiopia , Mali , Nigeria ,

items.title: Koulikoro : quatre morts dans un accident de la route
items.description: Quatre personnes ont trouve la mort dans un accident de la circulation survenu sur l'axe Koulikoro-Bamako, dans la nuit du 2 au 3 juillet. Selon des sources securitaires, leur moto est entree en collision avec un camion-benne. Parmi les victimes figure Maître Balla Sall, vice-president de la Federation malienne de karate et president des Ligues de karate et de Yoseikan Budo de Koulikoro. L’article Koulikoro : quatre morts dans un accident de la route est apparu en premier sur Studio Tamani - Informations, débats, magazines : toute l’actualité du Mali, en 5 langues .

items.title: Scandale au Mondial-2026: La Fifa blanchit Balogun après un coup de fil de Trump
items.description: Le Mondial-2026 tient son scandale: contrarié par la suspension de l'Américain Folarin Balogun pour le huitième de finale lundi contre la Belgique, Donald Trump a appelé le président de la Fifa Gianni Infantino, et le joueur a finalement été autorisé à jouer, une ingérence qui suscite un tollé. Deux sources proches du dossier ont confirmé dimanche à l'AFP que le président américain, qui vante de longue date son amitié avec le patron du football mondial, l'avait appelé mercredi pour demander le réexamen de la suspension de l'attaquant, quelques heures à peine après le 16e de finale contre la Bosnie-Herzégovine lors duquel Balogun avait été exclu. Et l'annonce, surréelle, est tombée dimanche par un communiqué de la Fifa: la commission de discipline a modifié la sanction. Le match de suspension ferme dont il avait automatiquement écopé a été commué en « un match de suspension avec sursis, assorti d'une période probatoire d'un an ». Conséquence: le meilleur buteur de Team USA dans cette Coupe du monde pourra bien être aligné à Seattle lors du huitièmes de finale face aux Diables rouges à 17h00 (mardi 00h00 GMT). « Merci à la Fifa d'avoir fait ce qu'il fallait et d'avoir réparé une grande injustice ! », a réagi quelques minutes seulement après l'annonce de la Fifa le président américain sur sa plateforme Truth Social. L'affaire a immédiatement viré au scandale, réveillant de vieux souvenirs d'autres ingérences politiques dans le plus grand tournoi de foot du monde. – « 1er avril » – Dans un communiqué, la fédération belge (URBSFA) a fait part de sa « stupéfaction » en rappelant que le code disciplinaire de la Fifa prévoyait qu'un carton rouge entraîne « automatiquement une suspension pour le match suivant ». « Il en a d'ailleurs été ainsi pour tous les cartons rouges distribués jusqu'à présent lors de cette Coupe du monde », observe-t-elle en relevant en outre que l'automaticité de la suspension avait été rappelée par la Fifa elle-même avant la compétition. La fédération a dit examiner « toutes les options potentielles ». Rudi Garcia, le sélectionneur français de la Belgique (à gauche) et Thibaut Courtois, le gardien des Diable rouges, en conférence de presse le 5 juillet 2026 à Seattle. "Je ne savais pas que le 5 juillet correspondait au 1er avril à la Fifa", a ironisé le premier " width="768" height="512" data-src="/resizer/v2/S5VKD3X55FFF3BW6V4SEHXNP7I.png?auth=f7de7396ae782d9e1067448e39a726cad814c6091c7a6c8532a5212a19f451ed" data-srcset="https://www.dhnet.be/resizer/v2/XOGH3EDDPFEX3A57RIQQZAZNIQ.jpg?auth=fb7983a01d293f6670a87d0f44f9de5334584b83124038b8086c721a81380866&width=768&height=512&quality=85&focal=384%2C256 768w" data-sizes="auto" data-optimumx="1" /> « Je ne savais pas que le 5 juillet correspondait au 1er avril à la Fifa », a déclaré le sélectionneur français des Diables rouges Rudi Garcia dimanche en conférence de presse, son gardien Thibault Courtois s'inquiétant d'un « précédent dangereux et bizarre ». « La vraie force, c'est de gagner avec fair-play (et en respectant toutes les règles). C'est ce que la Belgique fera demain (lundi) », a réagi sur X la ministre des Sports de la Fédération Wallonie-Bruxelles, Jacqueline Galant. « Allez les Diables, plus que jamais, tout le Pays est derrière vous. » « Shame on you (Honte à vous) ! Quand l'argent fait la loi, le mondial perd toute crédibilité », s'est offusqué sur X le parti socialiste belge (opposition). « Adapter les règles pour faire plaisir à Trump, tenter de tricher pour gagner, quelle image déplorable de la Fifa, du Mondial de foot et pour les Etats-Unis. » – Rubio scandalisé – La décision de la Fifa a en revanche été saluée par Team USA. « C'est une décision juste car il n'aurait pas dû y avoir carton rouge », a réagi le sélectionneur argentin de l'équipe, Mauricio Pochettino. « Nous ne sommes pas les victimes, mais nous ne sommes pas non plus les méchants dans cette affaire. » Folarin Balogun (au centre) avec Christian Pulisic (à gauche) lors d'une séance d'entraînement de l'équipe des Etats-Unis le 5 juillet 2026 à l'Université de l'Etat de Washington, à Seattle " width="768" height="512" data-src="/resizer/v2/S5VKD3X55FFF3BW6V4SEHXNP7I.png?auth=f7de7396ae782d9e1067448e39a726cad814c6091c7a6c8532a5212a19f451ed" data-srcset="https://www.dhnet.be/resizer/v2/XVSVUNQU6ZFAHNOGUZII7AGUGA.jpg?auth=ba96f3493815898fda9adaf4587cb8cb5019c745c87524f73f89abb8fc924768&width=768&height=512&quality=85&focal=384%2C256 768w" data-sizes="auto" data-optimumx="1" /> A voir si le fait d'être au coeur du premier scandale de cette Coupe du monde affectera, lundi, la prestation de Balogun, qui avait été exclu pour avoir marché sur une jambe du défenseur bosnien Tarik Muharemovic à la réception d'un saut. Vendredi, l'attaquant de Monaco avait expliqué aux journalistes que ce carton rouge était quelque chose qu'il devait « juste accepter ». Donald Trump s'était fait très discret depuis le début de cette Coupe du monde. Il s'était fendu d'un appel à Team USA pour l'encourager avant son entrée dans la compétition mais n'a assisté à aucun match. Il est cependant prévu qu'il remette avec Gianni Infantino le trophée au vainqueur de la finale le 19 juillet. L'affaire du carton rouge avait dès jeudi pris un tour politique, quand le chef de la diplomatie américaine, Marco Rubio, s'était lamenté de l'exclusion de Balogun en des termes peu diplomatiques. Les Etats-Unis « se sont fait entuber avec ce carton rouge », avait déclaré le secrétaire d'Etat lors d'une discussion informelle avec la presse. « Il faudrait une procédure d'appel pour ça. C'est probablement trop tard pour ça, n'est-ce pas? » AFP L’article Scandale au Mondial-2026: La Fifa blanchit Balogun après un coup de fil de Trump est apparu en premier sur Mediaguinee.com .
